name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
ثُمَّ بَعَثْنَا مِنۢ بَعْدِهِم مُّوسَىٰ وَهَـٰرُونَ إِلَىٰ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِۦ بِـَٔايَـٰتِنَا فَٱسْتَكْبَرُوا۟ وَكَانُوا۟ قَوْمًا مُّجْرِمِينَ (٧٥)
```

```english
Then, after them, We sent Moses and Aaron to Pharaoh and his elite with Our signs, but they acted arrogantly and they were a guilty lot. (75)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْحَقُّ مِنْ عِندِنَا قَالُوٓا۟ إِنَّ هَـٰذَا لَسِحْرٌ مُّبِينٌ (٧٦)
```

```english
When the truth from Us came to them, they said, ‘This is indeed plain magic!’ (76)
```

```arabic
قَالَ مُوسَىٰٓ أَتَقُولُونَ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جَآءَكُمْ ۖ أَسِحْرٌ هَـٰذَا وَلَا يُفْلِحُ ٱلسَّـٰحِرُونَ (٧٧)
```

```english
Moses said, ‘Do you say of the truth when it comes to you [that it is magic]? Is this magic? Magicians do not find salvation.’ (77)
```

```arabic
قَالُوٓا۟ أَجِئْتَنَا لِتَلْفِتَنَا عَمَّا وَجَدْنَا عَلَيْهِ ءَابَآءَنَا وَتَكُونَ لَكُمَا ٱلْكِبْرِيَآءُ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَمَا نَحْنُ لَكُمَا بِمُؤْمِنِينَ (٧٨)
```

```english
They said, ‘Have you come to us to turn us away from what we found our fathers following, so that supremacy may be yours in the land? We will not believe in the two of you.’ (78)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ٱئْتُونِى بِكُلِّ سَـٰحِرٍ عَلِيمٍ (٧٩)
```

```english
Pharaoh said, ‘Bring me every expert magician.’ (79)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّا جَآءَ ٱلسَّحَرَةُ قَالَ لَهُم مُّوسَىٰٓ أَلْقُوا۟ مَآ أَنتُم مُّلْقُونَ (٨٠)
```

```english
So when the magicians came, Moses said to them, ‘Throw down what you have to throw.’ (80)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّآ أَلْقَوْا۟ قَالَ مُوسَىٰ مَا جِئْتُم بِهِ ٱلسِّحْرُ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ سَيُبْطِلُهُۥٓ ۖ إِنَّ ٱللَّهَ لَا يُصْلِحُ عَمَلَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٨١)
```

```english
So when they threw down [their sticks and ropes], Moses said, ‘What you have produced is magic. Indeed Allah will bring it to naught presently. Indeed Allah does not foster the efforts of those who cause corruption. (81)
```

```arabic
وَيُحِقُّ ٱللَّهُ ٱلْحَقَّ بِكَلِمَـٰتِهِۦ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ ٱلْمُجْرِمُونَ (٨٢)
```

```english
Allah will confirm the truth with His words, though the guilty should be averse.’ (82)
```

```arabic
فَمَآ ءَامَنَ لِمُوسَىٰٓ إِلَّا ذُرِّيَّةٌ مِّن قَوْمِهِۦ عَلَىٰ خَوْفٍ مِّن فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَإِي۟هِمْ أَن يَفْتِنَهُمْ ۚ وَإِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعَالٍ فِى ٱلْأَرْضِ وَإِنَّهُۥ لَمِنَ ٱلْمُسْرِفِينَ (٨٣)
```

```english
But none believed in Moses except some youths from among his people, for the fear of Pharaoh and his elite that he would persecute them. For Pharaoh was indeed a tyrant in the land, and indeed he was an unrestrained [despot]. (83)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ يَـٰقَوْمِ إِن كُنتُمْ ءَامَنتُم بِٱللَّهِ فَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلُوٓا۟ إِن كُنتُم مُّسْلِمِينَ (٨٤)
```

```english
And Moses said, ‘O my people! If you have faith in Allah, put your trust in Him, if you have submitted [to Him].’ (84)
```

```arabic
فَقَالُوا۟ عَلَى ٱللَّهِ تَوَكَّلْنَا رَبَّنَا لَا تَجْعَلْنَا فِتْنَةً لِّلْقَوْمِ ٱلظَّـٰلِمِينَ (٨٥)
```

```english
Whereat they said, ‘In Allah we have put our trust.’ ‘Our Lord! Do not make us a [means of] test for the wrongdoing lot, (85)
```

```arabic
وَنَجِّنَا بِرَحْمَتِكَ مِنَ ٱلْقَوْمِ ٱلْكَـٰفِرِينَ (٨٦)
```

```english
and deliver us by Your mercy from the faithless lot.’ (86)
```

```arabic
وَأَوْحَيْنَآ إِلَىٰ مُوسَىٰ وَأَخِيهِ أَن تَبَوَّءَا لِقَوْمِكُمَا بِمِصْرَ بُيُوتًا وَٱجْعَلُوا۟ بُيُوتَكُمْ قِبْلَةً وَأَقِيمُوا۟ ٱلصَّلَوٰةَ ۗ وَبَشِّرِ ٱلْمُؤْمِنِينَ (٨٧)
```

```english
We revealed to Moses and his brother [saying], ‘Settle your people in the city, and let your houses face each other, and maintain the prayer, and give good news to the faithful.’ (87)
```

```arabic
وَقَالَ مُوسَىٰ رَبَّنَآ إِنَّكَ ءَاتَيْتَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَأَهُۥ زِينَةً وَأَمْوَٰلًا فِى ٱلْحَيَوٰةِ ٱلدُّنْيَا رَبَّنَا لِيُضِلُّوا۟ عَن سَبِيلِكَ ۖ رَبَّنَا ٱطْمِسْ عَلَىٰٓ أَمْوَٰلِهِمْ وَٱشْدُدْ عَلَىٰ قُلُوبِهِمْ فَلَا يُؤْمِنُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ يَرَوُا۟ ٱلْعَذَابَ ٱلْأَلِيمَ (٨٨)
```

```english
Moses said, ‘Our Lord! You have given Pharaoh and his elite glamour and wealth in the life of this world, our Lord, that they may lead [people] astray from Your way! Our Lord! Blot out their wealth and harden their hearts so that they do not believe until they sight the painful punishment.’ (88)
```

```arabic
قَالَ قَدْ أُجِيبَت دَّعْوَتُكُمَا فَٱسْتَقِيمَا وَلَا تَتَّبِعَآنِّ سَبِيلَ ٱلَّذِينَ لَا يَعْلَمُونَ (٨٩)
```

```english
Said He, ‘Your supplication has already been granted. So be steadfast, and do not follow the way of those who do not know.’ (89)
```

```arabic
۞ وَجَـٰوَزْنَا بِبَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ ٱلْبَحْرَ فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ وَجُنُودُهُۥ بَغْيًا وَعَدْوًا ۖ حَتَّىٰٓ إِذَآ أَدْرَكَهُ ٱلْغَرَقُ قَالَ ءَامَنتُ أَنَّهُۥ لَآ إِلَـٰهَ إِلَّا ٱلَّذِىٓ ءَامَنَتْ بِهِۦ بَنُوٓا۟ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ وَأَنَا۠ مِنَ ٱلْمُسْلِمِينَ (٩٠)
```

```english
We carried the Children of Israel across the sea, whereat Pharaoh and his troops pursued them, out of defiance and aggression. When overtaken by drowning, he called out, ‘I believe that there is no god except Him in whom the Children of Israel believe, and I am one of those who submit [to Him]!’ (90)
```

```arabic
ءَآلْـَٔـٰنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنتَ مِنَ ٱلْمُفْسِدِينَ (٩١)
```

```english
[He was told,] ‘What! Now? When you have been disobedient heretofore and were among the agents of corruption?! (91)
```

```arabic
فَٱلْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ ءَايَةً ۚ وَإِنَّ كَثِيرًا مِّنَ ٱلنَّاسِ عَنْ ءَايَـٰتِنَا لَغَـٰفِلُونَ (٩٢)
```

```english
So today We shall deliver your body so that you may be a sign for those who come after you.’ Indeed many of the people are oblivious to Our signs. (92)
```

```arabic
وَلَقَدْ بَوَّأْنَا بَنِىٓ إِسْرَٰٓءِيلَ مُبَوَّأَ صِدْقٍ وَرَزَقْنَـٰهُم مِّنَ ٱلطَّيِّبَـٰتِ فَمَا ٱخْتَلَفُوا۟ حَتَّىٰ جَآءَهُمُ ٱلْعِلْمُ ۚ إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِى بَيْنَهُمْ يَوْمَ ٱلْقِيَـٰمَةِ فِيمَا كَانُوا۟ فِيهِ يَخْتَلِفُونَ (٩٣)
```

```english
Certainly We settled the Children of Israel in a worthy settlement and We provided them with all the good things, and they did not differ until [after] the knowledge had come to them. Your Lord will indeed judge between them on the Day of Resurrection concerning that about which they used to differ. (93)
```

## (Commentary)

Then Allah, the Glorified, recounted the news of Moses, his brother and assistant (وزيره) Aaron, with Pharaoh and his assembly (ملئه). He summarized the story, yet presented it in a manner whose chapters correspond to the essence of the account of the Prophet’s (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) mission and his call to the tyrants of his people and the despots (الطواغيت) of Quraysh and others. It depicts their lack of faith in him—except for the weak among them whom they used to persecute until they were forced to migrate. Thus, he (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) and a group of the believers migrated to Medina, followed by the "Pharaohs" of this nation and their assembly, whom Allah then destroyed for their sins. Allah settled the believers, by the blessing of Islam, in a truthful settlement (مبوأ صدق) and provided them with good things (الطيبات). Then they differed after knowledge had come to them, and Allah will judge between them.

All of this served as a confirmation of what Allah, the Glorified, had privately disclosed to His Prophet (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) in these verses regarding the future events he and his people would face, and of his (صلى‌الله‌عليه‌وآله) saying as he addressed his companions and his nation: "You shall surely follow the ways (سنة) of the Children of Israel, to the extent that if they had entered a lizard's hole, you would enter it too."

**His saying, the Exalted:** «ثُمَّ بَعَثْنا مِنْ بَعْدِهِمْ مُوسى وَهارُونَ» — *Then We sent after them Moses and Aaron* (10:75) etc., meaning: then We sent, after Noah and the messengers who came after him, Moses and his brother Aaron with Our signs to Pharaoh and the group specifically associated with him from his people—who were the Copts—but they acted arrogantly against Our signs and were persistent in their criminality (الاجرام).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَلَمَّا جاءَهُمُ الْحَقُّ مِنْ عِنْدِنا» — *When the truth came to them from Us* (10:76) etc. It appears that what is meant by "the truth" (الحق) is the truthful sign (الآية الحقة), such as the serpent and the white hand. Allah made them both a sign for his [Moses'] messengership in truth. So when the truth came to them, they said, emphasizing their statement: "Indeed, this"—pointing to the truth of the sign—"is manifest sorcery (سحر مبين)," clear in its being magic. He only called the sign "truth" (حقاً) in contrast to their calling it "sorcery" (سحراً).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قالَ مُوسى أَتَقُولُونَ لِلْحَقِّ لَمَّا جاءَكُمْ أَسِحْرٌ هذا» — *Moses said, ‘Do you say [this] of the truth when it has come to you? Is this sorcery?* (10:77) etc. That is, when he heard that statement of theirs and their accusing the truth of being manifest sorcery, he said to them, denouncing their words in the form of a question: "Do you say of the truth when it has come to you" that it is sorcery? Then he repeated the denunciation with a question, saying: "Is this sorcery?" The object of the speech in the [first] interrogative sentence is omitted for brevity, as the second question indicates it. His saying: «وَلا يُفْلِحُ السَّاحِرُونَ» — *and the sorcerers do not prosper* (10:77) could be a circumstantial clause (جملة حالية) providing the reason for the denunciation indicated by his saying: "Is this sorcery?" Alternatively, it could be an independent statement explaining the reality, by which he exonerates himself from practicing sorcery, because he sees for himself prosperity (الفلاح) and for the sorcerers that they do not prosper.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قالُوا أَجِئْتَنا لِتَلْفِتَنا عَمَّا وَجَدْنا عَلَيْهِ آباءَنا» — *They said, ‘Have you come to us to turn us away from what we found our fathers following* (10:78) etc. "Turning away" (اللفت) is to divert from something. The meaning is: Pharaoh and his assembly said to Moses, rebuking him: "Have you come to us to turn us away" and divert us "from what we found our fathers following," meaning the way (سنة) and path of their ancestors, "and so that the two of you may have grandeur (الكبرياء) in the land?" They mean leadership, governance, the expansion of power, and the enforcement of will. They imply by this: "You two have taken the religious call as a means to nullify our established way in the land and to establish a new way of which you are the innovators and founders, so that by implementing it among the people and through our faith in you and obedience to you, you may possess grandeur and greatness in the kingdom."

In other words: "You have only come to replace the Pharaonic state, deeply rooted among the Copts, with an Israelite state managed by your leadership and guidance. We are not going to believe in you such that you achieve your desire and reach your goal through this forged call."

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَقالَ فِرْعَوْنُ ائْتُونِي بِكُلِّ ساحِرٍ عَلِيمٍ» — *Pharaoh said, ‘Bring me every learned sorcerer!’* (10:79). He was commanding his assembly to counter Moses' miracle with the sorcery of the magicians, as detailed in other verses recounting the story, and as indicated by the following verses.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَلَمَّا جاءَ السَّحَرَةُ قالَ لَهُمْ مُوسى أَلْقُوا» — *When the sorcerers came, Moses said to them, ‘Throw down* (10:80) etc. That is, when they arrived, faced Moses, and prepared to oppose him, Moses said to them: "Throw down what you are going to throw" of ropes and staffs, which they had prepared to throw so they would appear in the form of snakes and serpents through their sorcery.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَلَمَّا أَلْقَوْا قالَ مُوسى ما جِئْتُمْ بِهِ السِّحْرُ» — *When they had thrown, Moses said, ‘What you have brought is sorcery* (10:81). What he (peace be upon him) said was an exposition of one of the truths (الحقائق), so that what Allah would manifest of the truth at his hands—the staff becoming a serpent that swallows what they had thrown of ropes and staffs and displayed as snakes through their sorcery—would correspond to it.

The truth he explained to them is that what they brought is sorcery, and the nature of sorcery is to display that which is not an actual truth (حق واقع) in the form of an actual truth to the senses and sight of people. Since it is falsehood (باطل) in itself, Allah will surely nullify it, because the divine way (السنة الإلهية) proceeds by establishing the truth and its realization (إحقاقه) in the order of creation (التكوين), and by the perishing of falsehood and its nullification. Thus, the ultimate triumph (الدولة) belongs to the truth, even if falsehood has a temporary round (جولة).

Therefore, he justified his saying: «إِنَّ اللهَ سَيُبْطِلُهُ» — *Indeed Allah will soon nullify it* (10:81) with his saying: «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُصْلِحُ عَمَلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ» — *Indeed Allah does not further the work of the agents of corruption* (10:81). For "rectification" (الصلاح) and "corruption" (الفساد) are two opposite states. The divine way has proceeded to rectify what is righteous (صالح) and to let perish what is corrupt (فاسد); that is, to arrange for each of them its appropriate and specific effect. The effect of a righteous deed is that it suits and harmonizes with the rest of the cosmic truths in their system by which they proceed, blending and mixing with them; thus Allah, the Glorified, rectifies it and allows it to proceed according to its nature. The effect of a corrupt deed is that it does not suit or harmonize with the rest of the cosmic truths in what they require by their nature and proceed upon by their innate disposition (جبلتها). Thus, it is an exceptional matter in itself, and if Allah were to "rectify" it in its corruption, that would be a corruption of the cosmic system.

Consequently, the rest of the cosmic causes, with their influential powers and means, oppose it and return it to a righteous course if possible; otherwise, they nullify it, annihilate it, and erase it from the page of existence entirely.

This truth necessitates that sorcery, and every other falsehood, does not endure in existence. Allah, the Glorified, has established this in His speech in various places, such as His saying: «وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الظَّالِمِينَ» — *and Allah does not guide the wrongdoing people* (2:258), and His saying: «وَاللهُ لا يَهْدِي الْقَوْمَ الْفاسِقِينَ» — *and Allah does not guide the transgressing people* (5:108), and His saying: «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يَهْدِي مَنْ هُوَ مُسْرِفٌ كَذَّابٌ» — *Indeed Allah does not guide someone who is a profligate, a liar* (40:28). Among these is His saying in this verse: «إِنَّ اللهَ لا يُصْلِحُ عَمَلَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ» — *Indeed Allah does not further the work of the agents of corruption* (10:81).

He emphasized this by affirming it on the positive side in the following verse: «وَيُحِقُّ اللهُ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ» — *Allah confirms the truth by His words, though the guilty should be averse* (10:82), as the explanation will follow.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَيُحِقُّ اللهُ الْحَقَّ بِكَلِماتِهِ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ» — *Allah confirms the truth by His words, though the guilty should be averse* (10:82). When Allah disclosed the aforementioned truth on the negative side with His saying: "Indeed Allah does not further the work of the agents of corruption," He explained it on the positive side as well in this verse with His saying: "Allah confirms the truth by His words." He, the Exalted, combined the meanings of negation and affirmation in His saying: «لِيُحِقَّ الْحَقَّ وَيُبْطِلَ الْباطِلَ وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ» — *that He might confirm the truth and nullify falsehood, though the guilty should be averse* (8:8).

From here, the probability strengthens that what is meant by "the words" (الكلمات) in the verse are the types of divine decrees (الأقضية الإلهية) regarding the affairs of cosmic things that proceed according to the truth. For Allah's decree is effective, and His way is established: that He strikes the truth and falsehood against each other in the system of the universe, then falsehood does not last long before it perishes and its trace is effaced, while the truth remains in its clarity. That is His saying, the Exalted: «أَنْزَلَ مِنَ السَّماءِ ماءً فَسالَتْ أَوْدِيَةٌ بِقَدَرِها فَاحْتَمَلَ السَّيْلُ زَبَداً رابِياً وَمِمَّا يُوقِدُونَ عَلَيْهِ فِي النَّارِ ابْتِغاءَ حِلْيَةٍ أَوْ مَتاعٍ زَبَدٌ مِثْلُهُ كَذلِكَ يَضْرِبُ اللهُ الْحَقَّ وَالْباطِلَ فَأَمَّا الزَّبَدُ فَيَذْهَبُ جُفاءً وَأَمَّا ما يَنْفَعُ النَّاسَ فَيَمْكُثُ فِي الْأَرْضِ» — *He sends down water from the sky whereat the valleys are flooded to their capacity, and the flood carries a swelling scum. And from what they smelt in the fire for making ornaments or housewares, there is a similar scum. Thus does Allah draw the parable of truth and falsehood. As for the scum, it leaves as dross, and as for what profits the people, it remains in the earth* (13:17). A full discussion of this will come under that verse, if Allah wills.

In summary, Moses (peace be upon him) only mentioned this truth to them to make them aware of a true divine way (سنة إلهية) of which they were heedless, and to prepare their souls for what he would manifest in action—the victory of the miraculous sign over sorcery and the triumph of truth over falsehood. Therefore, they hastened to believe when they witnessed the miracle and threw themselves on the ground prostrating, as Allah, the Glorified, has detailed in other places of His speech.

His saying: «وَلَوْ كَرِهَ الْمُجْرِمُونَ» — *though the guilty should be averse* (10:82). He mentioned "guilt/criminality" (الاجرام) among their attributes because it carries the meaning of "severing" (القطع); it is as if they severed the path of truth for themselves and built their structure upon that. Thus, they are averse to the manifestation of the truth. For this reason, Allah attributed the aversion to the manifestation of truth to them insofar as they are "guilty" (مجرمون) in His saying: "though the guilty should be averse." Similar in meaning is His saying at the beginning of the verses: «فَاسْتَكْبَرُوا وَكانُوا قَوْماً مُجْرِمِينَ» — *but they acted arrogantly and they were a guilty people* (10:75).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَما آمَنَ لِمُوسى إِلَّا ذُرِّيَّةٌ مِنْ قَوْمِهِ عَلى خَوْفٍ مِنْ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَائِهِمْ» — *But none believed in Moses except some youths from among his people, for fear of Pharaoh and their [own] elders* (10:83) until the end of the two verses. Some commentators mentioned that the pronoun in "his people" (قومه) refers to Pharaoh, and the "youths" (الذرية) who believed from his people were those whose mothers were from the Children of Israel and whose fathers were from the Copts, so they followed their mothers in believing in Moses. It was also said: the youths were some of the children of the Copts. It was also said: what is meant by them are Pharaoh's wife and the believer from the family of Pharaoh—both mentioned in the Quran—and a slave girl and a woman who was the hairdresser of Pharaoh's daughter.

Others mentioned that the pronoun refers to Moses (peace be upon him), and what is meant by "youths" (الذرية) from Moses' people is a group from the Children of Israel who had learned sorcery and were among Pharaoh's companions. It was also said: they are all of the Children of Israel, who were six hundred thousand people, and he called them "youths" (ذرية) due to their weakness. It was also said: the descendants (ذرية) of the House of Israel from among those to whom Moses was sent, who had perished over the long period. These views—as you can see—have no evidence for any of them in the verses from a linguistic perspective.

What the context provides, and what is apparent from the verse, is that the pronoun refers to Moses, and what is meant by "youths" (الذرية) from Moses' people are some of the weak among the Children of Israel, excluding their powerful and noble elders (ملئهم). Reason supports this, for they were all captives of the Copts, governed by their rule. The common practice in such cases is for the nobles and the powerful to use any possible means to preserve their social status and national prestige, and to draw close to the dominant tyrant by pleasing him with wealth, outward service, feigning sincerity, and avoiding what he disapproves of. Thus, it was not within the power of the elders (الملأ) of the Children of Israel to announce their agreement with Moses' aim or to openly manifest faith in him.

Furthermore, the stories of the Children of Israel in the Quran are the best witness that many of the tyrants and arrogant ones among the Children of Israel did not believe in Moses until the end of his time, even if they submitted to him and obeyed him in his general commands issued to exert efforts for the salvation of the Children of Israel—since that involved the welfare of their nationality, the freedom of their people, and the interests of their individuals. Obedience in these matters is one thing, and faith in Allah and what the Messenger brought is another.

Based on this, the meaning of His saying: «وَمَلَائِهِمْ» — *and their [own] elders* (10:83) becomes sound, with the pronoun referring to the "youths" (الذرية). The speech implies that the weak youths, in their faith, feared the elders and nobles of the Children of Israel, for they might have prevented them because they themselves did not believe, or they feigned such opposition to please Pharaoh and his people and to appease them, so they would not restrict them or increase their persecution and pressure upon them.

As for what was said—that the pronoun refers to Pharaoh because he has companions, or to the youths because they were from the Copts—it is something that should not be adopted at all, especially the first of the two views.

His saying: «أَنْ يَفْتِنَهُمْ» — *lest he should persecute them* (10:83), meaning: punish them so they would return to his creed. His saying: «وَإِنَّ فِرْعَوْنَ لَعالٍ فِي الْأَرْضِ» — *Indeed Pharaoh was a tyrant in the land* (10:83), meaning: and the situation was such that Pharaoh was exalted in the land, a profligate (مسرف) in his affairs.

The meaning—and Allah knows best—is that it resulted from the story of their mission and the arrogance of Pharaoh and his assembly that none believed in Moses except the weak of the Children of Israel, while they feared their own elders and feared Pharaoh lest he punish them for their faith. It was expected and natural for them to fear, for Pharaoh was at that time exalted in the land, dominating them, and he was among the profligate (المسرفين) who do not act justly in what they judge and exceed the limit in oppression and torture.

If it were correct to mean by "his people" everyone to whom Moses was sent and to whom the message reached—namely the Copts and the Children of Israel—the speech would be sound in another way without the need for the aforementioned forced interpretations.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَقالَ مُوسى يا قَوْمِ إِنْ كُنْتُمْ آمَنْتُمْ بِاللهِ فَعَلَيْهِ تَوَكَّلُوا إِنْ كُنْتُمْ مُسْلِمِينَ» — *Moses said, ‘O my people! If you have believed in Allah, then put your trust in Him, if you have submitted [to Him]’* (10:84). Since faith in Allah—with what it provides the believer of knowledge of his Lord's station, even if in summary, and that He is the Cause above all causes to Whom every cause ends, and He is the Disposer of every affair—calls him to submit the matter to Him and to avoid relying on the outward means he can employ (for that is from ignorance), the necessary consequence of this is to refer the matter to Him and to rely (التوكل) upon Him. He commanded them in the verse to trust in Allah, linking it first to the condition of faith (الإيمان), then completing the speech with the condition of submission (الإسلام).

The speech is effectively: "If you have believed in Allah and are submissive to Him, then trust in Him." The two conditions were separated—and perhaps He did not combine them by saying, "If you have believed and submitted, then trust"—due to the difference between the two conditions according to the state. Faith was an established and verified fact from them, whereas submission (الإسلام) is from the perfection of faith. It is not a necessary obligation that every believer be a "submitter" (مسلم); rather, it is most fitting that he perfects his faith through submission.

Thus, the separation between the two conditions is to signal that one of them is an obligation already realized by them, and the other is what they ought to actualize. The meaning is: "O my people, if you have believed in Allah—and you have indeed believed—and if you are submissive to Him—as you ought to be—then trust in Allah." In the speech, there is a subtle literary craft that is not hidden.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَقالُوا عَلَى اللهِ تَوَكَّلْنا رَبَّنا لا تَجْعَلْنا فِتْنَةً لِلْقَوْمِ الظَّالِمِينَ» — *They said, ‘In Allah we have put our trust. Our Lord! Do not make us a temptation for the wrongdoing people* (10:85) until the end of the two verses. They only trusted in Allah so that He might save them from Pharaoh and his assembly. Thus, their supplication with what they prayed for in their saying: "Our Lord! Do not make us a temptation..." etc., is a request from them for the result of their trust, which is that Allah strip from them the garment of weakness and humiliation and save them from the faithless people.

As for the first, they indicated it by their saying: "Our Lord! Do not make us a temptation (فتنة) for the wrongdoing people." This is because what entices the strong wrongdoers against the weak oppressed is the weakness they observe in them; thus they are tempted (يفتنون) by it and oppress them. The weak person, by virtue of his weakness, is a temptation for the strong wrongdoer, just as wealth and children, by virtue of the attraction of love they possess, are a temptation for man. He, the Exalted, said: «إِنَّما أَمْوالُكُمْ وَأَوْلادُكُمْ فِتْنَةٌ» — *Your possessions and your children are only a temptation* (64:15). The world is a temptation for its seeker. So their asking their Lord not to make them a temptation for the wrongdoing people is a request for Him to remove weakness and humiliation from them by removing the purpose for it—which is removing a thing by removing its cause.

As for the second, namely salvation (التنجية), it is what He mentioned as a narration from them in the second verse: «وَنَجِّنا بِرَحْمَتِكَ مِنَ الْقَوْمِ الْكافِرِينَ» — *and deliver us by Your mercy from the faithless people* (10:86).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَأَوْحَيْنا إِلى مُوسى وَأَخِيهِ أَنْ تَبَوَّءا لِقَوْمِكُما بِمِصْرَ بُيُوتاً» — *We revealed to Moses and his brother [saying], ‘Settle your people in houses in Egypt* (10:87) etc. **Settling** (التبوؤ) is taking a dwelling and a residence. "Egypt" (مصر) is the land of Pharaoh. **Direction** (القبلة) is originally a noun of type (بناء نوع) from the root, like "sitting" (جلسة), meaning the state by which facing (التقابل) occurs between one thing and another. It is a verbal noun meaning the active participle; i.e., "make your houses facing each other, opposite one another and in one direction." The purpose was for the two of them to be able to reach them for propagation (التبليغ) and for them to be able to establish congregational prayer, as indicated or suggested by His saying after it: «وَأَقِيمُوا الصَّلاةَ» — *and maintain the prayer* (10:87), due to its occurrence after it.

As for His saying: «وَبَشِّرِ الْمُؤْمِنِينَ» — *and give good news to the believers* (10:87), the context indicates that what is meant is the good news of the answering of what they asked for in their aforementioned supplication: "Our Lord! Do not make us a temptation..." until the end of the two verses.

The meaning is: We revealed to Moses and his brother to take for your people residences of houses in Egypt—and it seems that until that time they were like Bedouins living in tents or a similar lifestyle—and you and your people make your houses facing each other and in one direction, so that you are connected to one another and the matter of propagation, consultation, and gathering for prayers can proceed. Establish the prayer, and you, O Moses, give good news to the believers that Allah will soon save them from Pharaoh and his people.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَقالَ مُوسى رَبَّنا إِنَّكَ آتَيْتَ فِرْعَوْنَ وَمَلَأَهُ زِينَةً وَأَمْوالاً» — *Moses said, ‘Our Lord! You have given Pharaoh and his elite finery and wealth* (10:88) etc. **Finery** (الزينة) is a noun of type from "adornment" (الزين), which is the form that attracts the soul to a thing. The relationship between finery and wealth is a general-particular overlap (عموم من وجه); some finery is not wealth for which a price is paid, such as a beautiful face or a well-proportioned stature, and some wealth is not finery, such as livestock and lands, while some wealth is finery, such as jewelry. The contrast occurring between finery and wealth suggests that what is meant by finery is the aspect of adornment without regard to its monetary value, such as jewelry, fine clothing (الرياش), furniture, and magnificent buildings, among others.

Regarding His saying: «رَبَّنا لِيُضِلُّوا عَنْ سَبِيلِكَ» — *Our Lord! that they may lead [people] astray from Your way* (10:88), it was said that the "Lām" (لـ) is for the consequence (لام العاقبة). The meaning is: "and the consequence of their affair is that they lead astray from Your way." It is not permissible for it to be the "Lām" of purpose (لام الغرض), because we have known through clear proofs that Allah, the Glorified, does not send a messenger to command the creation to go astray, nor does He desire misguidance for them; likewise, He does not give them wealth so that they may lead astray. [End of quote].

This is true regarding initial misguidance (الإضلال الابتدائي), which is impossible for Him, the Exalted. However, misguidance as a form of recompense (المجازاة) and meeting evil with evil—there is no proof for its impossibility for Allah, the Glorified; rather, His speech confirms it in many instances. Pharaoh and his assembly were persistent in arrogance and corruption, insistent on criminality; thus, there is no barrier to Allah giving them finery and wealth as a result, so that they may lead astray from His way as a recompense for what they earned.

It has been said that the "Lām" in "that they may lead astray" (ليضلوا) is for supplication (للدعاء), and it has been said that the speech implies a hidden "not" (لا), i.e., "lest they lead astray from Your way." The context does not support either of these two views.

"Effacing" (الطمس)—as has been said—is a change leading to obliteration and erasure. So the meaning of "efface their wealth" (اطمس على أموالهم) is: change it toward annihilation and disappearance. His saying: «وَاشْدُدْ عَلى قُلُوبِهِمْ» — *and harden their hearts* (10:88) is from "tightening" (الشد), the opposite of loosening; i.e., "harden their hearts and bind them with a binding such that they do not open to the truth, so they do not believe until they see the painful punishment." This is the "sealing" (الطبع) upon the hearts. The statement of some—that what is meant by "tightening" is fixing them in their place in Egypt after the effacing of their wealth so that it would be harder and more painful for them—and the statement of others—that it is a metaphor for their death and destruction—are among the far-fetched views.

The meaning of the verse is: Moses said—and this was after his despair of the faith of Pharaoh and his assembly and his certainty that they would only persist in misguidance and leading others astray, as indicated by the context of his speech in his supplication—"Our Lord! You have recompensed Pharaoh and his assembly for their faithlessness and defiance with an evil recompense, so You gave them finery and wealth in the life of this world. Our Lord! It is a will from You that they lead those who follow them astray from Your path, and Your will is not nullified and Your purpose is not in vain. Our Lord! Continue Your wrath upon them, efface their wealth and change it from the course of blessing to the course of vengeance, and make their hearts tightened and bound so they do not believe until they stand in a position where faith does not benefit them—which is the time they see the divine punishment."

This supplication from Moses (peace be upon him) against Pharaoh and his assembly only occurred after his total despair of their faith, and his knowledge that nothing was expected from them in life except that they go astray and lead others astray, similar to Noah's supplication against his people in what Allah narrated: «رَبِّ لا تَذَرْ عَلَى الْأَرْضِ مِنَ الْكافِرِينَ دَيَّاراً إِنَّكَ إِنْ تَذَرْهُمْ يُضِلُّوا عِبادَكَ وَلا يَلِدُوا إِلَّا فاجِراً كَفَّاراً» — *‘My Lord! Do not leave any of the faithless to dwell on the earth. Indeed if You leave them, they will lead Your servants astray, and will beget none but vicious ingrates’* (71:26-27). Far be it from the station of the Prophets (peace be upon them) that they should speak based on conjecture and suspicion in a position where they address the Lord of the worlds, majestic is His grandeur and mighty is His station.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «قالَ قَدْ أُجِيبَتْ دَعْوَتُكُما فَاسْتَقِيما وَلا تَتَّبِعانِّ سَبِيلَ الَّذِينَ لا يَعْلَمُونَ» — *He said, ‘The supplication of both of you has been granted. So be steadfast, and do not follow the path of those who do not know’* (10:89). The address—as indicated by the context—is to Moses and Aaron, yet the supplication in the previous verse was only narrated from Moses. This supports what the commentators mentioned: that Moses (peace be upon him) was supplicating, and Aaron was saying "Amen" to him, and the "Amen" is a supplication. Thus, they were both supplicating even if the text of the supplication was from Moses (peace be upon him) alone.

"Steadfastness" (الاستقامة) is firmness in a matter. From the two of them (peace be upon them), it is firmness in the call to Allah and in reviving the word of truth. What is meant by "those who do not know" (الذين لا يعلمون) are the ignorant among the people of Israel, and Moses (peace be upon him) described them as ignorant as in his saying: «قالَ إِنَّكُمْ قَوْمٌ تَجْهَلُونَ» — *He said, ‘You are indeed an ignorant people’* (7:138).

The meaning is: Allah "said," addressing Moses and Aaron: "The supplication of both of you has been granted" regarding the request for painful punishment for Pharaoh and his assembly, the effacing of their wealth, and the tightening of their hearts. "So be steadfast" and firm in what you have been commanded of the call to Allah and the revival of the word of truth, "and do not follow" at all "the path of those who do not know" by answering what they propose to you out of their own whims and the motives of their desires. In this, there is a kind of allusion that they would soon ask for matters involving the revival of their national customs and their pre-Islamic (جاهلية) ways.

In short, the verse mentions the answering of their supplication, which included the punishment of Pharaoh and his assembly and their lack of success in attaining faith, and it promises them that. Therefore, the following verse mentions His fulfillment of this promise with its specific details.

There was nothing in the supplication indicating a request for immediacy or delay in the judgment of punishment upon them. The context of the verse indicating acceptance and answering proceeded accordingly, as did the verse informing of the manner of its execution. It has been narrated in *al-Majma’*, from Ibn Jurayj: that Pharaoh remained for forty years after this supplication. He said: "And that was narrated from Abu ‘Abdillah (peace be upon him)." It was also narrated from him (peace be upon him) in *al-Ihtijaj*, and likewise in *al-Kafi*, and the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from Hisham ibn Salim from him (peace be upon him), and in the *Tafsir* of al-Qummi, from his father from al-Nawfali from al-Sakuni from him (peace be upon him).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَجاوَزْنا بِبَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ الْبَحْرَ فَأَتْبَعَهُمْ فِرْعَوْنُ وَجُنُودُهُ بَغْياً وَعَدْواً» — *We carried the Children of Israel across the sea, whereat Pharaoh and his hosts pursued them out of rebellion and hostility* (10:90) until the end of the verse. "Rebellion" (البغي) and "hostility" (العدو), like "aggression" (العدوان), are oppression. "Overtaking" (إدراك) a thing is catching up with it and gaining power over it, just as "pursuing" (إتباع) a thing is seeking to catch up with it.

His saying: «آمَنْتُ أَنَّهُ لا إِلهَ إِلَّا الَّذِي آمَنَتْ بِهِ بَنُوا إِسْرائِيلَ» — *‘I believe that there is no god except Him in whom the Children of Israel believe* (10:90), meaning: "I believe that He..."

He described Allah as "Him in whom the Children of Israel believe" so that he might attain what they attained through their faith—namely, crossing the sea and safety from drowning. For this reason also, he combined faith (الإيمان) and submission (الإسلام) to remove thereby the trace of what he had been persistent upon of sin—which was associating partners with Allah and arrogance against Allah. The rest is clear.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «آلْآنَ وَقَدْ عَصَيْتَ قَبْلُ وَكُنْتَ مِنَ الْمُفْسِدِينَ» — *[He was told,] ‘What! Now? When you have been disobedient before and were among the agents of corruption!* (10:91). "Now?" (آلآن) with the long vowel is originally "Is it now?" (ءألآن); i.e., "Do you believe in Allah now, when the punishment has overtaken you?" There is no faith or repentance when the punishment envelops and death comes from every place. "When you have been disobedient before this and were among the agents of corruption," and you spent your days in disobedience to Him and did not offer repentance in its time. So what benefit is faith to you after its time has passed? This is exactly what Moses and Aaron had asked their Lord: that He take him with a painful punishment and block his path to faith except when the punishment envelops him, at which point faith does not benefit him nor does repentance avail him anything.

**His saying, the Exalted:** «فَالْيَوْمَ نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ لِتَكُونَ لِمَنْ خَلْفَكَ آيَةً وَإِنَّ كَثِيراً مِنَ النَّاسِ عَنْ آياتِنا لَغافِلُونَ» — *So today We shall deliver your body so that you may be a sign for those who come after you. Indeed many of the people are oblivious of Our signs’* (10:92). "Delivering" (التنجية) and "saving" (الإنجاء) are the *Taf’īl* and *If’āl* forms from "salvation" (النجاة), like "releasing" (التخليص) and "sincerity/purification" (الإخلاص) from "freedom/purity" (الخلاص) in weight and meaning.

His delivering him "with his body" (ببدنه) indicates that he has another matter beyond the body which he lost when the punishment enveloped him—which is the soul (النفس), also called the spirit (الروح). This soul that is taken is the one that Allah takes away (يتوفاها) and seizes at the time of its death, as He, the Exalted, said: «اللهُ يَتَوَفَّى الْأَنْفُسَ حِينَ مَوْتِها» — *Allah takes the souls at the time of their death* (39:42), and He said: «قُلْ يَتَوَفَّاكُمْ مَلَكُ الْمَوْتِ الَّذِي وُكِّلَ بِكُمْ» — *Say, ‘The angel of death, who has been given charge of you, will take you’* (32:11). It is that about which a human says "I," and it is that by which human identity (إنسانية) is realized for a person. It is that which perceives, wills, and performs human acts through the medium of the body with its material powers and organs. The body is nothing but a tool and instrument with which the soul performs its material works.

Due to the union (الاتحاد) that exists between it and the body, the body is called by its name; otherwise, the names of persons are in reality for their souls, not for their bodies. Sufficient for you in this regard is the continuous change that affects the body during the period of life, and the natural transformation that occurs to it time after time, until perhaps the body is replaced in all its parts by other parts that compose another body. If "Zayd" were the body that his mother gave birth to on the day he was born and the name belonged to it, he would certainly be someone else when he is seventy or eighty, and the name would inevitably belong to someone else. Nor would a person be rewarded or punished in old age for what he did as a youth, because the obedience and disobedience would belong to someone else.

These and similar points are definitive witnesses that the humanity of a human is by his soul, not his body, and names are for the souls, not the bodies—a fact man perceives and knows in summary, even if he might deny it in detailed discussion.

In short, the verse: "So today We shall deliver you with your body" is like an explicit statement—or it is an explicit statement—that souls are beyond bodies, and that names are for the souls rather than the bodies, except for what is applied to the bodies by virtue of the union.

So the meaning of "We shall deliver you with your body" (نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ) is: "We shall bring your body out of the painful [punishment] and save it." This is a type of delivering "you"—due to the union between the soul and the body which dictates that an act occurring to one of them occurs in a way to the other—"so that you may be a sign for those who come after you." This is, in a way, similar to His saying, the Exalted: «مِنْها خَلَقْناكُمْ وَفِيها نُعِيدُكُمْ» — *From it We created you, and into it We will return you* (20:55). For that which is returned to the earth is the corpse of the human, not the complete human; thus, the attribution of the return to the human is only due to the union between his soul and his body.

Commentators have mentioned that since "delivering" (الإنجاء) and "saving" (التنجية) indicated by their wording the safety of the one who was saved, the implication of His saying: "We shall deliver you" (نُنَجِّيكَ) would be that Pharaoh came out of the painful [punishment] alive, yet Allah brought him out dead. Thus, it is necessary to take His saying: "We shall deliver you" (نُنَجِّيكَ) from "the high ground" (النجوة), which is the elevated land that the flood does not reach. The meaning would be: "Today We shall bring your body out to a high ground of the earth."

Some of them perhaps said that what is meant by "the body" (البدن) is the coat of mail, and Pharaoh had a coat of mail made of gold by which he was known, so Allah brought him out above the water with his coat of mail to be a sign and a lesson for those after him; and some said that the expression of "delivering" is a form of sarcasm (تهكم) toward him.

The truth is that all of this is forced interpretation for which there is no need. He did not say: "We shall deliver you" (نُنَجِّيكَ) [alone]; rather, it was said: "We shall deliver you with your body" (نُنَجِّيكَ بِبَدَنِكَ), and its meaning is: "We shall deliver your body." The "Bā" (بـ) is for instrumentality or causality, and the underlying rationale is the union that exists between the soul and the body.

Furthermore, making "We shall deliver you with your body" mean "We shall place you on a high ground of the earth" does not suffice to repel the objection from its root. For that which was placed on a high ground was Pharaoh's body according to their view, and it is certainly other than Pharaoh—otherwise he would be alive and safe. There is no escape except to say: that is by virtue of the union that exists between the human and his body. If this rationale justifies applying the name of the human to his body without the soul, it would also justify attributing the "delivering" to the human by virtue of the delivering occurring to his body—especially with the presence of the context indicating that what is meant by the delivering is that which is for the body, not that which is for the human which entails the preservation of his life and safety in soul and body. The context is His saying: "with your body" (بِبَدَنِكَ).

**His saying, the Exalted:** «وَلَقَدْ بَوَّأْنا بَنِي إِسْرائِيلَ مُبَوَّأَ صِدْقٍ وَرَزَقْناهُمْ مِنَ الطَّيِّباتِ» — *Certainly We settled the Children of Israel in a worthy settlement and We provided them with all the good things* (10:93). That is, We settled them in a settlement of truth (مبأ صدق). A thing is only attributed to "truth" (الصدق)—such as a truthful promise (وعد صدق), a truthful footing (قدم صدق), a truthful tongue (لسان صدق), a truthful entrance (مدخل صدق), and a truthful exit (مخرج صدق)—to indicate that the necessities of its meaning and its desired effects are truthfully present in it, without it being false in any of its effects which it promises by the tongue of its implicative indication (دلالته الالتزامية) to its seeker. A truthful promise, for example, is the promise that its promiser will fulfill, and the one promised will be delighted by its fulfillment, and it is right that it be craved and its occurrence hoped for. If it is not so, then it is not a truthful promise, but rather a false promise, as if it lies in its meaning and the necessities of its meaning.

Accordingly, His saying: "a worthy settlement" (مُبَوَّأَ صِدْقٍ) indicates that Allah, the Glorified, settled them in a settlement where all that a human seeks from a residence is found—of the purposes of dwelling such as good water and air, the blessings of the earth, the abundance of its favors, stability therein, and so on. These are the regions of the Holy House (بيت المقدس) and the Levant (الشام) in which Allah settled the Children of Israel and called it the Holy Blessed Land, and the Quran has recounted their entry into it.

As for the statement of some—that what is meant by this settlement is Egypt, which the Children of Israel entered and took houses therein—it is a matter the Quran did not mention. Furthermore, even if their second entry into it were assumed, they did not settle there with a continuous stability, and calling what is of this nature a "truthful settlement" is something the meaning of the word does not support.

The verse—meaning His saying: "Certainly We settled the Children of Israel..." until His saying "...with all the good things"—is presented in the manner of a complaint and a rebuke. This is evidenced by its conclusion with His saying: «فَمَا اخْتَلَفُوا حَتَّى جاءَهُمُ الْعِلْمُ» — *and they did not differ until knowledge had come to them* (10:93). His saying: «إِنَّ رَبَّكَ يَقْضِي بَيْنَهُمْ» — *Indeed your Lord will judge between them* (10:93) until the end of the verse is an exposition of the consequence of their differing out of knowledge, and it is in the position of drawing the conclusion from the story.

The meaning is: We completed the favor upon the Children of Israel, settled them in a truthful settlement, and provided them with good things after their long deprivation of that while they were in the captivity of the Copts. We unified their people and gathered their scattered ranks, yet they were ungrateful for the favor, divided the word, and differed regarding the truth. Their differing was not due to the excuse of ignorance; rather, they differed out of knowledge. Indeed, your Lord will judge between them regarding that in which they used to differ.
